item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This really does bring back childhood memories! I have made cardboard spaceships and time machines before! Who else has done so as a kid?

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

SCP-2986 is a Gulf Canon skip, and could likely do with a link back to its hub, as well as the other articles and tales in that hub, of which there are many. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

SCP-2986 is a cardboard box that was originally utilized in the delivery of a Whirlpool refrigeration unit (addressed to Jupiter, Florida). When SCP-2986 is entered and sealed, the object will take on the appearance of the interior of a space-faring vessel. Technology within the vessel is beyond that of current human development, but appears to be designed for the use of humanoid individuals.

To this:

SCP-2986 is a cardboard box that was originally utilized in the delivery of a Whirlpool refrigeration unit (addressed to Jupiter, Florida). When SCP-2986 is entered and sealed, the object will take on the appearance of the interior of a space-faring vessel. Technology within the vessel is beyond that of current human development, but appears to be designed for the use of humanoid individuals.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really, really good, but it feels incomplete somehow. The story you've mentioned in the discussion posts could really stand to be in the article more, perhaps a few more logs or something. +1 anyway because it's caught in my head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For people confused about how to get out and/or the airlock:

From what I understand, the box and the vessel are effectively two different objects. Entering the box puts you in the spaceship, in which there is a decompression chamber that allows you to exit the ship into the rest of the imaginary universe. The decompression chamber is not connected to or related to the box. As for exiting the box, I think that it does have to be opened from outside once closed, since it doesn't seem like Billy knew how to get out. So the Foundation would either hold it open (assuming the ship is still accessible if it is open) or just set time limits for exploration and open the box to let everyone out. Either opening the box from outside pops everyone (and things on their immediate person) from the ship into the box, or opening it creates a portal at the open end that is connected to a square portal somewhere in the ship (whether on a bulkhead or just floating somewhere midair).

+ Regarding Mary
- Maybe spoilery

Since the other people were all imagined by Billy, when Mary told Billy she wanted to turn around, he got mad and deleted her from existence, hence the lack of body or accessible logs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man at first I thought oh a light hearted calvin and hobbes SCP, and then…man reality bender got himself lost in space and killed. That got ultra tragic quick.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah that was poor reading comprehension on my part. Sorry.

I'm still all confused by the clocks though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The document says it travels FTL, not that it claims to travel FTL. This should be a Thaumiel. It's a pretty reliable time machine as written…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
what I can figure...
...otherwise got me

So Billy made copies of his friends. I can kind of see why. "Come inside, check out my rocketship!" "Man, that's kid-stuff. So uncool." So he made copies and took them with him on whatever wonderful adventures he could dream up. Guess he ran out of dreams along the way.

One thing I surmised from the way it was written (others seem to have this impression) is that the box, once closed, then becomes the alternate reality. Whoever's inside is trapped there, the only exit to "space". Unless a third party opens the box from outside. Might also stress earlier in the description it is bigger on the inside. I didn't catch that until the logs.

+1 for when the dream outlives the dreamer. Elegantly done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed! This is so close to being amazingly good!

Novote for now, but I'll be watching for updates…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you may be overthinking things. This is a child's imaginary rocket ship. It doesn't have to behave according to actual physics. The Foundation knows it goes faster than light because the imaginary instruments say it does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it a lot, but it really feels like it's missing some info. A logbook, and flightplan, personal effects, something.

give me more!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think maybe the velocity measuring problem may be being a little overthought. Could there be a speedometer calibrated in multiples of c (light speed)? That's the sort of thing a child might include, I think.

I agree that this is very similar to the magic bus, but maybe I like this better. Hmmm…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In which case how does the Foundation meaningfully measure it as superuminal by dropping stuff overboard and accelerating away from it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My guess is that the preteen reality warper probably didn't know about general relativity, and so it didn't exist in his pocket universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How do you know how long the trip takes, except with clocks in your superuminal reference frame?

How did ~50 years pass in that reference frame anyway?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did not get that, no. I assumed the other children were real…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

Oh hah. Yeah. That was in an earlier draft and got dropped (it was a glock 17 that could be removed, but turned into a squirt gun afterwads). In that case I'm not sure why anyone would think that no one can escape. Unless they didn't quite catch that Billy is just a reality warper and nothing and noone else inside the box is real outside of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There wasn't any mention of a squirt gun in the article, just a gunshot wound.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some spoilers:

+ show block
– hide block

Some things can leave, as outlined in the piece. And the way out is clear now that the reality bender in charge of the pocket universe has killed himself.

The reason why everyone else and most objects (except stuff like the squirt gun which was brought in from outside by Billy) can't leave is because billy created them all. We can go in and leave easily, it's much bigger inside than out, and the door in and out is easily opened (as long as you're "real" outside of the pocket universe).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Except if the object is moving superlimully and you are, then information about the object might never reach you or you might reach the information before you drop the object or all sorts of FTL weirdness…

Also how does the Foundation know all this stuff if nothing can leave the ship's universe?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You drop an object and it would be moving with regards to the reference frame, yes. But then you pick a direction and send the ship there. Regardless of the reference frame you'll leave it behind and it will be a measurable distance away.

If you also know how long the trip took, bam… you know your velocity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wouldn't the objects dropped also be moving at superuminal velocities? With all the inherent problems with that reference frame?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty much. Also if you can't figure out how to measure the velocity of such a ship with no external reference points you're not thinking hard enough.

You drop something out the back then move. It's such a trivial thing that I didn't think it even needed to be mentioned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That doesn't explain how The Foundation gets information out of it, or how The Foundation knows it travels at FTL speeds. That's where this breaks for me. I'm reading an SCP document about this, someone had to get information out in order to write the document, and somebody had to measure velocity realitve to some reference frame in order to specify it in said document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This whole box became a spaceship through the imagination of kids (at least that's how I read it); needless to say, I think their imaginations— while full of pretty cool ideas if I say so myself — left some things out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How does information leave the spaceship? Can you return to the "real world" by some means once entered? In which case why didn't the original children ever leave?

Also if there are no stars or other reference points how can you measure the velocity of the ship?

Downvoting due to seemingly irreconcilable plot holes…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This definitely feels like a more succinct version of 1958.

I do not disapprove.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept, but I feel there's a piece of important information that is missing: How could the Foundation find this object? I mean, how could they locate an anomalous Whirlpool cardboard box between every Whirlpool cardboard box that when inside turns into a vessel if its previous, and presumably only, crew have been missing for over 50 years?

Also:

The vessel can be exited through manual operation of a decompression chamber located in the ships rear. While outside of the vessel one is subject to vacuum.

If I understand it correctly, the way for somebody inside of 2986 to get out is by unsealing it from the outside? If not, how were the remains retrieved?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me of SCP-1958, the Magic Bus. But it has its own twists, which I like. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Makes me think of a fucked up version of Calvin and Hobbes. I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like I'm missing something? I like it, but it just doesn't really do anything for me. Sorry. No vote from me for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes. This is wonderful.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a collaboration between myself and Faminepulse. Most of the credit oughta rightfully be theirs, given that this was their idea originally, I just put a little bit of work into it.

Edit: For clarity, by the way, this fits all the requirements for a contest entry, but I'm not planning on making an entry so I've posted it too early to be considered.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Original draft and concept by me, story elements, hook, and pacing heavily revised (and introduced) by Cimmerian.

This is not a contest entry.
